blocks.content: Nathan left around two. The apartment settled back into its afternoon quiet — the particular quality of it after someone has been there and then gone, which is a different quiet than the kind that precedes anyone arriving.
Ethan sat for a while without doing anything. He looked at the sketchbooks. He looked at the pencils. He looked at the window.
His phone lit up on the counter.
Okay I need an opinion. There's a breakfast place on Meridian that has been on my list for months and I keep not going and I think today might be the day. Have you been?
He picked up the phone. He read it twice. Something in him loosened slightly — the way a thing loosens when the thing you've been worrying about turns out to be writing you first.
The one with the green awning?
Yes. That one. I walk past it every time I go to the pharmacy and I always think 'next time' and then next time arrives and I keep walking.
I've been. The eggs are very good. The coffee is aggressively mediocre.
Aggressively mediocre is an incredible phrase. I'm going. Will report back.
I want the full review. Category breakdown. What worked, what didn't, whether the coffee lived up to its reputation.
I'll do a formal assessment. Maybe a rubric.
A weighted rubric. Atmosphere should count for at least twenty percent.
Agreed. Green awnings automatically get extra points. I have a bias and I'm disclosing it.
He was smiling. He was aware that he was smiling. He looked at the phone and then set it face-up on the table in case she sent another one, and then picked up a pencil.
Also — I was thinking about you today. Not for a specific reason. Just generally. Is that allowed?
He set the pencil back down.
Good. Then it was intentional and not an accident at all.
